_note: Glossary file for JOC Cockpit — Japanese. Each key is lowercase with hyphens (e.g. 'daily-plan'). Values support markdown including nested ^glossary terms^.

glossary.joc-cockpit:
**JOC Cockpit** is the user interface and the REST API server of JS7.

It is used to manage the ^Inventory^ of ^Workflow^s and ^Job^s, to monitor and to control job execution.

JOC Cockpit offers access to the job history and to logs.

JOC Cockpit can be operated from an single instance and from a ^JOC Cockpit Cluster^.
glossary.joc-cockpit-instance-id:
The **JOC Cockpit Instance ID** is a unique number that specifies the instance in a ^JOC Cockpit Cluster^.

All JOC Cockpit instances in the same cluster use a different Instance ID.

The *JOC Cockpit Instance ID* is specified during installation from an ascending number starting from 0.

JOC Cockpit instances are displayed in the *DASHBOARD* view in ascending order by Instance ID.
glossary.joc-cockpit-cluster-id:
The **JOC Cockpit Cluster ID** is a unique identifier that specifies a ^JOC Cockpit Cluster^.

All JOC Cockpit instances in a cluster use the same identifier.

The *JOC Cockpit Cluster ID* is specified during installation from a string such as `joc` for the GUI combined with the REST API Server and `api` for a REST API Server without GUI.
glossary.joc-cockpit-cluster:
The **JOC Cockpit Cluster** includes a number of ^JOC Cockpit^ instances for high availability with automated fail-over and manual switch-over.

All JOC Cockpit instances in a cluster use the same ^JOC Cockpit Cluster ID^ and an individual ^JOC Cockpit Instance ID^.

Membership in a *JOC Cockpit Cluster* is specified during installation.
glossary.controller:
The **Controller** is the orchestration component of JS7.

It manages ^Workflow^ execution, distributes ^Order^ processing to ^Agent^ nodes, and maintains the overall cluster state.

The Controller can be operated from a
- ^Autonomous Controller^: single instance
- ^Controller Cluster^: two active-passive instances.
glossary.autonomous-controller:
An **Autonomous Controller** is operated from a single instance.

A ^Controller Cluster^ is operated from two instances in active-passive clustering for high availability.
glossary.controller-cluster:
A **Controller Cluster** includes one active and one standby ^Controller^ instance in active-passive mode for high availability.

With clustering being established the active Controller instance's transactional data will be replicated to the standby Controller instance.

The Cluster supports automated fail-over and manual switch-over.
glossary.controller-id:
The **Controller ID** identifies an ^Autonomous Controller^ or ^Controller Cluster^.

The *Controller ID* cannot be changed during the lifetime of a Controller.

In a Controller Cluster all instances are configured using the same *Controller ID*
glossary.agent:
An **Agent** is the remote ^Job^ execution component registered with a ^Controller^.

It runs ^Job Script^s in the operating system and Jobs in the Java Virtual Machine. The Agent reports back results to the Controller.

Agents come in two flavors:
- ^Autonomous Agent^ from a single instance
- ^Agent Cluster^ from two active-passive ^Director Agent^s and a number of ^Subagent^s
glossary.agent-id:
The **Agent ID** is the unique identifier of an ^Agent^ per ^Controller^. It cannot be changed through the lifetime of an Agent.

For ^Deployment^ of objects, the ^Agent Name^ is used that can be the same across scheduling environments such as *DEV*, *UAT* and *PROD*.
glossary.agent-name:
The **Agent Name** is the unique name of an ^Agent^ per ^Controller^ used for ^Deployment^. The *Agent Name* can be modified at any time as opposed to the ^Agent ID^.

Users can add Alias Names that are variants of the *Agent Name*. The *Agent Name* or its Alias Names are assigned to ^Job^s and ^File Order Source^s.

If the *Agent Name* changes, such objects cannot be deployed. Users can
- Update the *Agent Name* using ^Inventory Search^.
- assign the Agent an *Alias Name* holding the previous *Agent Name*.

It is good practice to use the same *Agent Names* across scheduling environments such as *DEV*, *UAT* and *PROD*
glossary.autonomous-agent:
**Autonomous Agents** execute ^Job^s on remote machines, on-premises and from containers.

They are operated individually and are managed by a ^Controller^.
glossary.agent-cluster:
An **Agent Cluster** includes:
- ^Director Agent^s: orchestrating Subagents and executing ^Job^s.
- ^Subagent^s: executing Jobs.

An *Agent Cluster* offers high availability for job execution with automated fail-over and manual switch-over.

An *Agent Cluster* is identified by its ^Agent Cluster Name^.

A number of *Agent Clusters* can be managed by a ^Controller^.
glossary.agent-cluster-name:
The **Agent Cluster Name** is a unique identifier for the ^Agent Cluster^.

It cannot be changed during the Agent Cluster's lifetime.
glossary.subagent:
**Subagents** execute ^Job^s on remote machines, on-premises and from containers.

They are considered worker nodes in an ^Agent Cluster^ and are managed by ^Director Agent^s.
glossary.subagent-id:
The **Subagent ID** is the unique identifier of a ^Subagent^. It cannot be changed through the lifetime of a Subagent.

For ^Deployment^ of objects, the ^Subagent Cluster ID^ is used that can be the same across scheduling environments such as *DEV*, *UAT* and *PROD*.
glossary.subagent-cluster:
**Subagent Clusters** include the
- **Selection** of ^Director Agent^s and ^Subagent^s in an ^Agent Cluster^
- **Sequence** in which Subagents will be assigned for execution of next ^Job^s.
  - **active-active**: each next Job will be executed with the next Subagent. All selected Subagents are involved. For details see - [JS7 - Agent Cluster - Active-Active Subagent Cluster](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Agent+Cluster+-+Active-Active+Subagent+Cluster).
  - **active-passive**: the first Subagent will be used for Job execution. If it is unavailable, then the next Subagent will be used. For details see [JS7 - Agent Cluster - Active-Passive Subagent Cluster](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Agent+Cluster+-+Active-Passive+Subagent+Cluster).
  - **metrics-based**: depending on the number of processes, CPU and memory consumption, the next Subagent will be selected for Job execution. For details see [JS7 - Agent Cluster - Metrics-based Subagent Cluster](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Agent+Cluster+-+Metrics-based+Subagent+Cluster).
glossary.subagent-cluster-id:
The **Subagent Cluster ID** is the unique identifier of a ^Subagent Cluster^. It cannot be changed during the Subagent Cluster's lifetime.

It is recommended using the same *Subagent Cluster ID* across environments such as *DEV*, *UAT*, *PROD*.
glossary.director-agent:
**Director Agents** orchestrate ^Subagent^s in an ^Agent Cluster^.

They are operated from two instances in active-passive clustering with automated fail-over and manual switch-over.

*Director Agents* are managed by a ^Controller^.

In addition to orchestration of Subagents, they can be used to execute ^Job^s.
glossary.director-agent-id:
The **Director Agent ID** is a unique identifier for a ^Director Agent^ instance.

It cannot be changed during the Director Agent's lifetime.
glossary.order:
**Orders** trigger execution of ^Workflow^s.

They specify a start time, carry parameters and track the current execution position in the Workflow.

Orders are identified by their ^Order ID^.

Orders are stateful, see [Order States](context:help:order-states).
glossary.order-name:
The **Order Name** is an optional name in the ^Order ID^ that can be used to filter ^Order^s in a number of views.

The *Order Name* can be freely chosen and is limited to 30 characters. Users who wish longer qualifiers can apply ^Tag^s.
glossary.order-id:
The **Order ID** is created when adding an ^Order^. It identifies the Order from the `#<yyyy-mm-dd>#<qualifier>-<order-name>` naming scheme:
- **yyyy-mm-dd**: the ^Daily Plan^ date to which the Order belongs
- **qualifier**: the Order's origin:
  - **F**: added from a ^File Order Source^
  - **C**: added from a ^Schedule^ to the Daily Plan for cyclic starts
  - **P**: added from a Schedule to the Daily Plan for single start
  - **T**: added by users on demand
- **order-name**: an optional name, see ^Order Name^
glossary.job:
A **Job** is the smallest unit of work within a ^Workflow^.

The ^Agent^ executes a ^Job Script^ in the operating system or a Job in the Java Virtual Machine.

It reports the Job outcome (success, failure, or warning) and log output back to the ^Controller^.
glossary.exit-code:
The numeric **Exit Code** is returned by the OS for a given command or for the last command in a set of commands of a ^Job Script^.

By default *Exit Code* 0 signals success, non-zero *Exit Codes* signal failure.

*Exit Codes* can be used for ^Workflow^ control and can be checked by ^If Instruction^s to decide about subsequent ^Job^s.
glossary.return-code:
The numeric **Return Code** is returned by a ^Job^ in two flavors:
- ^Exit Code^ of a Shell Job
- *Return Code* of a Java Job running in the Java Virtual Machine
glossary.return-variable:
The **Return Variables** of a ^Job^ are provided from dynamic variables that are created by the Job and that are accessible to subsequent Jobs and instructions in a ^Workflow^.
- **Shell Jobs**: create *Return Variables*, for example,
  -  **Unix**: `echo "myVariable=myValue" >> $JS7_RETURN_VALUES`
  - **Windows**: `echo myVariable=myValue >> %JS7_RETURN_VALUES%`
- **Java Jobs**: create *Return Variables* by use of the JS7 Job API.
glossary.inventory:
The **Inventory** is the configuration store for ^JOC Cockpit^ where objects are managed.
- **Controller**: Objects ^deployed|deployment^ to ^Controller^s
  - ^Workflow^s
  - ^File Order Source|file-order-source^s
  - ^Job Resource^s
  - ^Notice Board^s
  - ^Resource Lock^s
- **Automation**: Objects ^released|releasing^ in JOC Cockpit
  - ^Script Include^s
  - ^Schedule^s
  - ^Calendar^s
  - ^Job Template^s
  - ^Report Template^s
glossary.deployment:
**Deployment** is the process of publishing ^Inventory^ objects and dependent objects to one or more ^Controller^s, making them available for execution.
- ^Workflow^s
- ^File Order Source^s
- ^Job Resource^s
- ^Notice Board^s
- ^Resource Lock^s
glossary.releasing:
**Releasing** is the process by which *Automation Objects* and dependent objects in the ^Inventory^ are made available for use.
- ^Script Include^s
- ^Schedule^s
- ^Calendar^s
- ^Job Template^s
- ^Report Template^s
glossary.daily-plan:
The [Daily Plan](context:help:daily-plan) holds ^Order^s for execution of ^Workflow^s at given dates.

Orders are created from ^Schedule^s by the ^Daily Plan Service^.

Users can modify Order start times and parameterization for individual dates.
glossary.daily-plan-service:
The [Daily Plan Service](context:help:service-daily-plan) creates and submits ^Order^s from the ^Daily Plan^ to ^Controller^s.

The service is operated in the background and acts on a daily basis to plan and to submit Orders a number of days ahead.
glossary.change:
A **Change** is a revision of inventory objects that are modified for common ^Deployment^, for example, a set of ^Workflow^s and related ^Resource Lock^s.

Users can add inventory objects directly to a *Change*.

In addition, *Changes* automatically include any referencing objects and referenced objects.
glossary.notification:
**Notifications** are configured from the *CONFIGURATION -> Notification* page:
- The *MONITOR -> Order Notifications* and *MONITOR -> System Notifications* sub-views display *Notifications*.
- Notifications can be sent by mail.
- Notifications can be processed from the command line, for example, invoking the agent of a System Monitor tool.
glossary.inventory-search:
**Inventory Search** offers use of meta characters:
- `?` meta character replaces any single character.
- `*` meta character replaces zero or more characters.

Search is performed case-insensitive and partially qualified, for example:
- `rest` will find objects with the name *pdfNon**Rest**artable* and ***REST**-RunningTaskLog*
- `re?t` will find objects with the name *ActivePassiveDi**rect**or* and *JITL-JS7**REST**ClientJob*
- `re*t` will find objects with the name *pdSQLExecuto**rExt**ractJSON* and *pdu**Reset**Subagent*

For details see [Inventory Search](context:help:searching-inventory).
glossary.pending:
**Pending** ^Order^s are added to ^Workflow^s without start time, they can be assigned a start time later.

Such Orders can be suspended or cancelled.

For details see [Order States](context:help:order-states).
glossary.scheduled:
**Scheduled** ^Order^s are added to ^Workflow^s for execution at a future date and time.

Such Orders can be suspended or cancelled.

For details see [Order States](context:help:order-states).
glossary.in-progress:
**In Progress** ^Order^s are processed by ^Workflow^ instructions but are not running a ^Job^.

Such Orders can be suspended or cancelled.

For details see [Order States](context:help:order-states).
glossary.running:
**Running** ^Order^s are in execution of a ^Job^.

Such Orders can be suspended or cancelled.

For details see [Order States](context:help:order-states).
glossary.suspended:
**Suspended** ^Order^s have been halted by user intervention and can be resumed or cancelled.

For details see [Order States](context:help:order-states).
glossary.completed:
**Completed** ^Order^s did terminate but did not leave the ^Workflow^.

For example, if a ^File Order Source^ is used for file watching and the Workflow will not (re)move the incoming file.

In this situation the Order will remain in place as long as the incoming file exists. It will vanish automatically if the incoming file no longer exists.

For details see [Order States](context:help:order-states).
glossary.prompting:
**Prompting** ^Order^s are put on hold by the ^Prompt Instruction^ in a ^Workflow^ and require user confirmation to continue execution of the Workflow.

Such Orders can be suspended or cancelled.

For details see [Order States](context:help:order-states).
glossary.waiting:
**Waiting** ^Order^s wait for a resource such as a ^Resource Lock^, ^Notice^, ^Retry Instruction^ or ^Cycle Instruction^ interval or for a process should the ^Agent^ in use specify a process limit that is exceeded.

Such Orders can be suspended or cancelled.

For details see [Order States](context:help:order-states).
glossary.blocked:
**Blocked** ^Order^s cannot start, for example, if the ^Agent^ is not reachable since the Order was added or if the ^Workflow^ is suspended.

Blocked Orders will automatically start provided that the reason no longer exists.

For details see [Order States](context:help:order-states).
glossary.failed:
**Failed** ^Order^s indicate that a ^Job^ failed or that a ^Fail Instruction^ prevents the Order from proceeding.

Failed Orders can be resumed or cancelled.

For details see [Order States](context:help:order-states).
glossary.planned:
**Planned** ^Order^s have been created in the ^Daily Plan^ but have not been ^submitted^ to the ^Controller^ and ^Agent^s. They are picked up by the ^Daily Plan Service^ that automatically submits *planned* Orders.

*Planned* Orders are visible in the *DAILY PLAN* view.

Users can manually submit or remove *planned* Orders.
glossary.submitted:
**Submitted** ^Order^s have been ^planned^ by the ^Daily Plan Service^ and have been forwarded to the ^Controller^ and ^Agent^s that will start Orders autonomously.

*Submitted* Orders are visible in the *DAILY PLAN* view.

The *submitted* status applies to Orders scheduled for future execution and to Orders in execution.

Users can cancel *submitted* Orders that will fall back to the *planned* state.
glossary.finished:
**Finished** ^Order^s have been completed.

*Finished* Orders are visible in the *DAILY PLAN* view.

The *HISTORY -> Orders* view explains if execution was successful or failed.
glossary.workflow:
A **Workflow** is a [directed acyclic graph](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Directed_acyclic_graph) of ^Job^ nodes connected by instructions.

It defines the execution logic, branching, error handling, and order of operations for automated tasks.

Workflows are started by ^Order^s.
glossary.file-order-source:
A **File Order Source** monitors a directory from an ^Agent^ for incoming files and adds an ^Order^ to a ^Workflow^ for each file.

^Job^s in Workflows move or remove an incoming file.
glossary.job-resource: **Job Resources** hold variables from key/value pairs that are provided from arguments in JVM ^Job^s and from environment variables in Shell Jobs.
glossary.notice-board: A **Notice Board** is a named communication channel. ^Order^s can post and can expect ^Notice^s to synchronize execution across different ^Workflow^s.
glossary.resource-lock:
A **Resource Lock** is a mutual-exclusive resource that limits access, for example, concurrent ^Job^ executions from ^Order^s accessing the shared resource in the same or in different ^Workflow^s.

The ^Lock Instruction^ in a Workflow determines the *Resource Locks* being used.

*Resource Locks* are used, for example, to limit parallel access of Jobs to databases.
glossary.script-include:
**Script Includes** hold code snippets for use with ^Job Script^s.

For example, ^Job^s for scripting languages such as Shell, Python, PowerShell can store reusable functions and modules in *Script Includes* that are merged with the Job when the ^Workflow^ is ^deployed|deployment^, see ^usage|script-include-usage^.

For details see [JS7 - Script Includes](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Script+Includes).
glossary.script-include-usage:
Jobs can reference ^Script Include^s like this:
- When displaying the ^Job Script^ in the GUI, users can hit the Ctrl+Space key to select and to embed a *Script Include*.
- The Job Script is added a line like this: `##!include pdScriptBashFunctions`
- When the ^Workflow^ is deployed, the line is expanded to hold the content of the Script Include.

For details see [JS7 - Script Includes](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Script+Includes).
glossary.job-template:
**Job Templates** are centrally managed templates for ^Job^s used in ^Workflow^s.

They are applied if the same Job implementation is used for a number of Jobs.
glossary.calendar:
**Calendars** define a set of dates, working days, non-working days, or custom periods.

Calendars are used by ^Schedule^s to calculate dates for which ^Order^s will be created.
glossary.schedule:
**Schedules** define the temporal rules for when ^Order^s should be started that trigger execution of ^Workflow^s.

They make use of ^Calendar^s to determine dates and they specify start times, ^Time Zone^s, and repeat intervals.
glossary.report-template:
**Report Templates** specify which data will be processed when creating a report.

*Report Templates* can be parameterized with the number of hits and the sorting (highest/lowest).

*Report Templates* indicate which ^Report Model^ should be used for the report.
glossary.report-model:
The **Report Model** selects from a list of predefined models frequently used for reports, for example:
- *Top 10 Workflows with highest number of failed executions*
- *Top 10 Jobs with highest number of failed executions*
- *Top 10 Agents with highest number of parallel Job executions*

The *Report Model* is added to the ^Report Template^.
glossary.date-formula:
**Date formulas** allow entering dates and times relative to the current time, e.g., `1w`, `12h`:
- **s** Second
- **m** Minute
- **h** Hour
- **d** Day
- **w** Week
- **M** Month
- **y** Year

If combined entries are allowed, you can enter *01:30:10* in the format `1h 30m 10s`.
glossary.object-naming-rules:
**Object Names** can be up to 255 Unicode characters long.

Most Unicode characters can be used, dots and hyphens, however, no spaces, no leading or trailing dots or hyphens.

For details see
- [JS7 - Object Naming Rules](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Object+Naming+Rules)
- [JS7 - Unicode Support](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Unicode+Support)
glossary.customization:
**Customizations** define filters for views that display lists of objects. Filters can be persisted and can be called up.

Customizations can be shared with other users of ^JOC Cockpit^ for read access.
glossary.documentation:
Users can create **Documentation** for objects such as ^Workflow^s and ^Job^s from plain text, markdown, HTML, PDF and other formats.

Documentations can be uploaded from the *RESOURCES -> Documentations* page. They are displayed from the WORKFLOWS view.

For details see [JS7 - User Documentation](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+User+Documentation).
glossary.job-script:
**Job Scripts** hold commands executed with ^Job^s:
- **Shell Jobs** run commands available from the OS including Shell scripts and binary files, for example, for interpreters such as Python or PowerShell.
- **JavaScript JVM Jobs** execute JavaScript code with the JVM (GraalVM).
- **Python JVM Jobs** execute Python code with the JVM (GraalVM).

Job Scripts can incorporate ^Script Include^s.

For details see [JS7 - Job Classes](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Job+Classes).
glossary.job-class:
The **Job Class** specifies the type of a ^Job^:
- **Shell Jobs** execute ^Job Script^s holding OS commands.
- **JITL Jobs** are Java Jobs that ship with the ^Agent^.
- **Java Jobs** are created by users using the JS7 Job API.
- **JavaScript JVM Jobs** execute JavaScript code with the JVM (GraalVM).
- **Python JVM Jobs** execute Python code with the JVM (GraalVM).

For details see [JS7 - Job Classes](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Job+Classes).
glossary.tag:
**Tags** are offered for ^Workflow^s, ^Order^s and ^Job^s for the purpose of:
- selecting Workflows in a number of views.
- selecting Orders in a number of views.
- adding qualifiers to Jobs for monitoring purposes.

*Tags* can be managed by ^Tag Group^s.

For details see [JS7 - Tagging](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Tagging).
glossary.tag-group:
**Tag Groups** are used to organize ^Tag^s.

When using the syntax `<tag-group>:<tag>`, for example `Priority:low` then the `low` Tag is created as a member of the `Priority` *Tag Group*.

Most views offer navigation and search by Tags and Tag Groups.
glossary.order-priority:
If an ^Order^ executes a ^Lock Instruction^ in the ^Workflow^ that limits parallelism, then its **Order Priority** determines the position in the queue of ^waiting^ Orders.

*Order Priorities* are specified from negative, zero and positive integers or from the shortcuts offered. A higher *Order Priority* has precedence:
- **Low**: -20000
- **Below Normal**: -10000
- **Normal**: 0
- **Above Normal**: 10000
- **High**: 20000
glossary.admission-times:
**Admission Times** limit access of ^Order^s to ^Job^s and other instructions by time:
- Jobs: can be configured for execution in certain timeslots. This includes periods during the day, weekdays, days of month and months. Optionally, Jobs can be skipped in case that an Order's date will not match a timeslot.
- ^AdmissionTimes Instruction^: is a block instruction that holds any number of ^Workflow^ instructions to which *Admission Times* will be applied.
glossary.posting-order:
A **Posting Order** is an ^Order^ that executes a ^PostNotices Instruction^ that will post a ^Notice^ to one or more ^Notice Board^s.

Such Notices are waited for by ^Expecting Order^s.
glossary.expecting-order:
An **Expecting Order** is an ^Order^ that executes an ^ExpectNotices Instruction^ or ^ConsumeNotices Instruction^ that will wait for a ^Notice^ to one or more ^Notice Board^s.

Notices are created from ^Posting Order^s.
glossary.notice: A **Notice** is created by a ^Posting Order^ for a given ^Notice Board^. Such *Notices* are waited for by ^Expecting Order^s.
glossary.notice-id:
The **Notice ID** is the unique identifier of a ^Notice^. It can hold the ^Daily Plan^ date to specify the Notice's scope for a given date.

^Order^s in ^ExpectNotices Instruction^s and ^ConsumeNotices Instruction^s wait for the arrival of Notices whose ID contains the same date from the ^Daily Plan^ as the Order.
glossary.regular-expression: **Regular expressions** are patterns for strings that are used to validate input or to extract strings, see [Regular expression](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Regular_expression)
glossary.time-zone:
**Time Zones** are areas that observe a common distance to [Coordinated Universal Time](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coordinated_Universal_Time) (UTC).

*Time Zones* are added from their identifier such as `UTC`, `Europe/London`, `Europe/Paris`, `US/pacific` etc., see [List of tz database time zones](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_tz_database_time_zones).
glossary.function:
**Functions** can be used in assignments to arguments and environment variables, for example:
- `env('HOME')`: returns the value of the `HOME` environment variable.
- `now(format='yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ssZ', timezone='Europe/London')` returns the current time in the format specified.

For details see [JS7 - Expressions for Variables](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Expressions+for+Variables).
glossary.cyclic-order:
For **Cyclic Orders** related ^Schedule^s create individual ^Order^ instances for each period in a cycle.

Running a cycle every 30s during the day counts to 2880 daily Orders for a single ^Workflow^. Users should be aware of
- load caused by a high number of Orders.
- error handling being applied individually per Order.
- ^Notification^s being created individually per Order in case of errors or warnings.

In most situations use of ^Cyclic Workflow^s from a ^Cycle Instruction^ is preferable and offers more options.
glossary.cyclic-workflow:
**Cyclic Workflows** make use of a ^Cycle Instruction^ and are started by a single ^Order^.

The Order waits for begin of the cycle and repeats the instructions inside the block:
- no load is caused by repeated cycles.
- error handling applies to a single Order.
- ^Notification^s are created for a single Order.

As opposed to *Cyclic Workflows* users can configure ^Schedule^s to create ^Cyclic Order^s.
glossary.identity-service:
**Identity Services** are set up to authenticate and authorize users.

The ^Identity Service Type^ determines access to the Identity Provider.

If multiple *Identity Services* are configured, they
- are used in the specified order until authentication with an *Identity Service* is successful.
- are used as the first or second factor for authentication if configured accordingly.
glossary.identity-service-type:
**Identity Service Types** govern access to the Identity Provider for an ^Identity Service^:
- ^Certificate Identity Service^
- ^FIDO Identity Service^
- ^JOC Identity Service^
- ^Keycloak Identity Service^
- ^LDAP Identity Service^
- ^OIDC Identity Service^

The KEYCLOAK-JOC, LDAP-JOC and OIDC-JOC *Identity Service Types* manage roles with JOC Cockpit. The similar *Identity Service Types* without *-JOC* Suffix hand-over role assignment to JOC Cockpit during authentication.
glossary.certificate-identity-service:
The **Certificate** ^Identity Service^ requires a Client Authentication Certificate created for the user account, it does not make use of a password.

The certificate's subject specifies the user account from the CN (common name) attribute.

^JOC Cockpit^'s truststore must include the Root CA Certificate and Intermediate CA Certificate that was used to sign the Client Authentication Certificate.

The Identity Service is frequently used for the purpose of:
- **Single Factor** for ^Job^s that connect to JOC Cockpit to use its REST API.
- **Second Factor** for authentication.

For details see [JS7 - Certificate Identity Service](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Certificate+Identity+Service)
glossary.fido-identity-service:
The **FIDO** ^Identity Service^ is a standard based on [WebAuthn](https://www.w3.org/TR/webauthn-1/) that offers two flavors:
- **Roaming Authenticator** is a secure way to store the user's Private Key on a personal device, for example, on a USB stick or smartphone.
- **Platform Authenticator** aka *Passkeys* store the user's Private Key on the local machine and typically will synchronize keys with the vendor's cloud.

For details see [JS7 - FIDO Identity Service](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+FIDO+Identity+Service).
glossary.joc-identity-service:
The **JOC** ^Identity Service^ stores the user account and hashed password in the JS7 database.

This requires managing user accounts individually with JOC Cockpit.

This includes that roles are managed with JOC Cockpit.

For details see [JS7 - JOC Identity Service](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+JOC+Identity+Service).
glossary.keycloak-identity-service:
The **Keycloak** ^Identity Service^ connects to the Red Hat product for authentication and authorization.

Keycloak manages identities from a number of Identity Providers such as LDAP, local accounts etc.

For details see [JS7 - Keycloak Identity Service](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Keycloak+Identity+Service).
glossary.ldap-identity-service:
The **LDAP** ^Identity Service^ is used with Active Directory and other products implementing the LDAP protocol.

For details see [JS7 - LDAP Identity Service](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+LDAP+Identity+Service).
glossary.oidc-identity-service:
The **OIDC** ^Identity Service^ is a popular standard for Identity Providers such as Azure, Google etc.

The following prerequisites apply to configuration of the JOC Cockpit application with the Identity Provider:
- JOC Cockpit must be configured as a *Single Page Application* (SPA). No other application types such as *Web* must be configured.
- The *Redirect URL* must include the hostname, port and */joc* path, for example, ^https://myHost:4446/joc^. This similarly applies when using a Proxy Service.

For details see [JS7 - OIDC Identity Service](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+OIDC+Identity+Service).
glossary.blocklist:
The **Blocklist** includes a number of user accounts that are denied access to JOC Cockpit.

Operations for adding and removing user accounts from the *Blocklist* are offered from the *MANAGE IDENTIFY SERVICES* page.
glossary.security-level:
^JOC Cockpit^ can be operated in one of the security levels:
- ^Low Security Level^
- ^Medium Security Level^
- ^High Security Level^

The *Security Level* cannot be changed within the lifetime of JOC Cockpit. Changes require reinstalling JOC Cockpit.
glossary.low-security-level:
The **Low** ^Security Level^ automatically signs ^Inventory^ objects by JOC Cockpit using the `root` account's Private Key and Signing Certificate.

^Deployment^ is easy to use from a single-click operation as signing operations are performed without user interaction.

There is no certainty about who has deployed objects, as any user who is authorized to deploy objects can use the operation from a single mouse click.

The `root` account's Private Key is stored in the JS7 database and is accessible to an administrator.
glossary.medium-security-level:
The **Medium** ^Security Level^ automatically signs ^Inventory^ objects by JOC Cockpit using the user account's Private Key and Signing Certificate.

This mechanism is similar to implicit signing except for the fact that the Private Key and Signing Certificate stored with the current user's profile is used.

Note that similarly to implicit signing, all Private Keys of user accounts are stored in the JS7 database and are accessible to a DBA or system administrator.
glossary.high-security-level:
The **High** ^Security Level^ requires ^Inventory^ objects being exported and being signed individually outside of the JOC Cockpit.

This guarantees that at any point in time, the JOC Cockpit has no knowledge of the Private Key used for signing.

There is some effort required to export Inventory objects, to sign and to import/deploy the signed objects.
glossary.job-instruction:
The **Job Instruction** implements a ^Job^. ^Workflow^s can include any number of Jobs.

For details see [JS7 - Job Instruction](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Job+Instruction).
glossary.try-catch-instruction:
The **Try-Catch Instruction** implements exception handling from a *Try* block that holds ^Job^s and other instructions.

If a Job or instruction fails, then the instructions in the *Catch* block will be executed.

An empty *Catch* block will resolve the error status of a previously failed instruction.

For details see [JS7 - Try-Catch Instruction](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Try-Catch+Instruction).
glossary.retry-instruction:
The **Retry Instruction** repeats execution of a sequence of ^Job^s and other instructions in case of failure.

If one of the Jobs in the *Retry* block fails, then the ^Order^ is moved to the begin of the *Retry* block to repeat execution.

For details see [JS7 - Retry Instruction](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Retry+Instruction).
glossary.finish-instruction:
The **Finish Instruction** makes an ^Order^ leave the ^Workflow^ with a successful or unsuccessful outcome in the [JS7 - Order History](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Order+History).

For details see [JS7 - Finish Instruction](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Finish+Instruction).
glossary.fail-instruction:
The **Fail Instruction** makes an ^Order^ fail. Without further error handling the Order will remain in the ^failed^ state, see [Order States](context:help:order-states).

A surrounding ^Try-Catch Instruction^ or ^Retry Instruction^ is triggered by the *Fail Instruction*.

For details see [JS7 - Fail Instruction](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Fail+Instruction).
glossary.fork-instruction:
The **Fork Instruction** allows ^Order^s being forked and joined to enable parallel processing of ^Job^s and other instructions in a ^Workflow^.

Branches are created by dragging & dropping instructions on the *Fork Instruction*. When an Order enters the *Fork Instruction*, then a Child Order is created for each branch.
- Each Child Order will pass the nodes in its branch independently of parallel Child Orders.
- Child Orders can return results to Parent Orders by passing variables.
- Child Orders take the role of Parent Orders in nested *Fork Instructions*.

For details see [JS7 - Fork-Join Instruction](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Fork-Join+Instruction).
glossary.forklist-instruction:
The **ForkList Instruction** is the dynamic version of a ^Fork Instruction^ and comes in the following flavors:
- The instruction expects an ^Order^ providing a *List Variable* that holds a list (array) of values.
  - The instruction is designed as a single branch: for each element in the *List Variable*, the ^Agent^ will dynamically create a branch.
  - This allows, for example, executing ^Job^s for each entry of a *List Variable*.
  - For details see [JS7 - ForkList-Join Instruction](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+ForkList-Join+Instruction).
- The instruction allows a number of Child Orders and branches being created dynamically and executing the same sequence of Jobs or other instructions on a number of ^Subagent^s.
  - Users can run the same Jobs in parallel on all Subagents of a ^Subagent Cluster^.
  - For details see [JS7 - ForkList-Join Instruction for Agent Clusters](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+ForkList-Join+Instruction+for+Agent+Clusters).
glossary.cycle-instruction:
The **Cycle Instruction** repeats execution of all or some ^Job^s and other instructions in a ^Workflow^.

It's a block instruction that can spawn the complete Workflow or selected Jobs and instructions. The *Cycle Instruction* can be nested.

The following cycle types are offered:
- **periodical**: using individual start times per period
- **continuous**: applying a fixed pause between end and start of cycles
- **ticking**: using a fixed start time interval

For details see [JS7 - Cycle Instruction](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Cycle+Instruction).
glossary.break-instruction:
The **Break Instruction** is used inside a ^Cycle Instruction^ block to terminate the cycle and to make an ^Order^ leave the cycle, frequently in combination with an ^If Instruction^.

For details see [JS7 - Break Instruction](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Break+Instruction).
glossary.lock-instruction:
The **Lock Instruction** is a block instruction that is used to specify one or more ^Job^s and other instructions for mutual exclusion, to prevent Jobs from being executed in parallel in the same ^Workflow^ or in different Workflows.

The instruction acquires one or more ^Resource Lock^s and can be *exclusive* or *shared* allowing a configurable number of parallel executions of Jobs and instructions in the block.

*Lock Instructions* can be nested.

For details see [JS7 - Lock Instruction](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Lock+Instruction).
glossary.sleep-instruction:
The **Sleep Instruction** is used to delay further processing in a ^Workflow^ by an amount of time specified in seconds.

For details see [JS7 - Sleep Instruction](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Sleep+Instruction).
glossary.prompt-instruction:
The **Prompt Instruction** halts execution of an ^Order^ in a ^Workflow^ until the prompt is confirmed. The Order is assigned the ^prompting^ state.

Users can confirm, suspend or cancel prompting Orders, see [Order States](context:help:order-states).

For details see [JS7 - Prompt Instruction](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Prompt+Instruction).
glossary.admissiontimes-instruction:
The **AdmissionTimes Instruction** halts execution of an ^Order^ in a ^Workflow^ until the given timeslot is reached. The Order is assigned the ^waiting^ state.

Orders can be terminated if they exceed the given timeslot.

The instruction can be configured to skip all instructions included in case that no matching timeslot is found for the Order's start date.

For details see [[JS7 - AdmissionTimes Instruction](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+AdmissionTimes+Instruction).
glossary.addorder-instruction:
The **AddOrder Instruction** is used in a ^Workflow^ to create an ^Order^ for a different Workflow.

By default such Orders run asynchronously in a separate Workflow and in parallel to the current Order, i.e. their execution result is not synchronized and does not have an impact on the current Order.

Should execution of Orders be synchronized, then the ^ExpectNotices Instruction^ and ^ConsumeNotices Instruction^ can be used.

For details see [JS7 - AddOrder Instruction](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+AddOrder+Instruction).
glossary.postnotices-instruction:
The **PostNotices Instruction** is used to create ^Notice^s for ^Notice Board^s.

The Notices are waited for by the ^ExpectNotices Instruction^ and ^ConsumeNotices Instruction^ in the same or in different ^Workflow^s.

A Workflow can include any number of *PostNotices Instructions* to post Notices to the same or to different Notice Boards.

Posting a Notice will not block further execution of an ^Order^ in a Workflow.

For details see [JS7 - PostNotices Instruction](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+PostNotices+Instruction).
glossary.expectnotices-instruction:
The **ExpectNotices Instruction** is used to wait for ^Notice^s becoming available from ^Notice Board^s that have been added by a ^PostNotices Instruction^ or by the user.

If the Notice does not exist, then the ^Order^ will remain in the ^waiting^ state with the instruction.

A Workflow can include any number of *ExpectNotices Instructions* to expect Notices from the same or from different Notice Boards.

For details see [JS7 - ExpectNotices Instruction](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+ExpectNotices+Instruction).
glossary.consumenotices-instruction:
The **ConsumeNotices Instruction** is used to make ^Order^s wait for ^Notice^s in ^Notice Board^s that are added by a ^PostNotices Instruction^ or by the user.

The *ConsumeNotices Instruction* is a block instruction that can include any other instructions and that will delete the Notices when an Order reaches the end of the instruction block.

For details see [JS7 - ConsumeNotices Instruction](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+ConsumeNotices+Instruction).
glossary.if-instruction:
The **If Instruction** is a block instruction used for conditional processing in a ^Workflow^.

It allows checking ^Return Code^s and ^Return Variable^s of previous ^Job^s and evaluation of ^Order^ variables.

For details see [JS7 - If Instruction](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+If+Instruction).
glossary.case-instruction:
The **Case Instruction** is used for conditional processing of ^Job^s and other instructions in a ^Workflow^. The instruction extends the ^If Instruction^.

The *Case Instruction* can be used with repeated ^CaseWhen Instruction^s and optionally with a single ^CaseElse Instruction^.

For details see [JS7 - Case Instruction](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Case+Instruction).
glossary.casewhen-instruction:
The **CaseWhen Instruction** is used to check a predicate similar to the ^If Instruction^.

The instruction can occur any number of times in a ^Case Instruction^.
glossary.caseelse-instruction: The **CaseElse Instruction** is used if all checks from ^CaseWhen Instruction^s fail.
glossary.stickysubagent-instruction:
The **StickySubagent Instruction** can be used to execute a number of ^Job^s with the same ^Subagent^ of an ^Agent Cluster^.

The block instruction checks the first available Subagent of a ^Subagent Cluster^. The same Subagent will be used for subsequent Jobs within the block instruction.

For details see [JS7 - StickySubagent Instruction](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+StickySubagent+Instruction+for+Agent+Clusters).
glossary.options-instruction:
The **Options Instruction** is a block instruction that rules error handling, for example, for the ^Lock Instruction^ and ^ConsumeNotices Instruction^.

If the *Options Instruction* is in place and specifies the *Stop on Failure* property then ^failed^ ^Order^s will remain with the failing instruction, for example, a ^Job^.

If the instruction is not in place, then Orders that fail within a block instruction will be moved to the begin of the instruction block and will remain in the failed state.

For details see [JS7 - Options Instruction](https://kb.sos-berlin.com/display/JS7/JS7+-+Options+Instruction).
